title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read an article from which six sentences have been removed. Choose from the sentences A–G the one which fits each gap (37–42). There is one extra sentence which you do not need to use.

content.title: After Dark: The World of Asian Night Markets
content.text: As the sun sets over Taipei, the city comes alive in a different way. Lanterns flicker on, grills start to smoke and thousands of vendors take their positions behind counters piled high with food, clothing and curious objects. (37) [37] Esta escena se repite en muchas ciudades de Asia cada noche. [/37] Night markets are a cornerstone of daily life across much of East and Southeast Asia, from Taiwan and Thailand to Malaysia and Vietnam. || The origins of these markets stretch back centuries. In Tang Dynasty China, official markets were forced to close at dusk, and merchants who wished to continue trading after dark did so informally, in the flickering light of lanterns. (38) [38] Con el tiempo, estos intercambios nocturnos se convirtieron en una tradición arraigada. [/38] What began as an economic workaround evolved over generations into a cultural institution that no government directive could easily dismantle. || Food is always the centrepiece. The sheer variety on offer at a single market can be staggering: grilled squid, mango sticky rice, stinky tofu, scallion pancakes and freshly squeezed sugar cane juice jostle for space on the same narrow lane. (39) [39] La comida de estos mercados no es solo sustento, sino también identidad cultural. [/39] Many dishes sold at night markets are hyper-local specialities that home cooks rarely attempt, making the market an irreplaceable repository of culinary knowledge. || Beyond food, night markets function as social hubs. Families stroll, teenagers gather and elderly neighbours play chess at fold-out tables. (40) [40] El ambiente festivo fomenta el encuentro entre personas de todas las edades. [/40] In societies where apartment living means limited private space, the night market becomes, in effect, a communal living room open to all. || Night markets have also captured the imagination of international tourists. Lonely Planet and TripAdvisor rank markets such as Shilin in Taipei and Chatuchak in Bangkok among Asia's top attractions. (41) [41] El turismo ha transformado algunos mercados tradicionales en destinos internacionales. [/41] This visibility has brought investment and infrastructure improvements, though some longtime vendors worry that rising rents and sanitised presentation are eroding the authentic spirit of the experience. || The tension between preservation and progress is real. (42) [42] Las autoridades locales buscan un equilibrio entre modernizar y conservar la esencia original. [/42] Cities like Penang have introduced 'heritage market' designations that protect certain vendors from displacement, recognising that a night market is not merely commerce but living culture.

paragraphs.A: Over time, these informal gatherings solidified into a tradition too deeply rooted to disappear.
paragraphs.B: The narrow lanes, fragrant smoke and colourful stalls have inspired countless food writers and documentary makers.
paragraphs.C: Local authorities are now trying to balance modernisation with the preservation of cultural authenticity.
paragraphs.D: A similar scene unfolds nightly in dozens of cities from Seoul to Kuala Lumpur.
paragraphs.E: For many vendors, the dishes they serve represent family recipes passed down through several generations.
paragraphs.F: Tourism has turned some of the most famous markets into international landmarks.
paragraphs.G: The festive, open-air atmosphere encourages people of all generations to linger and connect.

questions.explanation: D generalises the Taipei scene to other Asian cities, serving as a bridge towards the broader description of the night market.
A explains how those informal exchanges became tradition, connecting the historical anecdote with the following sentence about cultural evolution.
E adds the cultural dimension of food by mentioning family recipes, before the statement about the market as a culinary repository.
G describes the social and festive atmosphere that brings people of all ages together, linking to the idea of the market as a communal living room.
F introduces the impact of tourism, preparing the mention of specific markets and the concerns of traditional vendors.
C describes the authorities' effort to find a balance, connecting with the Penang example that closes the article.
